Private Thought of Kobayashi: Alright, new project! This is pretty exciting, a truly blank slate where we can really let our imaginations run wild. "Free ideas," "outside the box"... that's the kind of challenge I enjoy. Science fiction is our department, but it sounds like we don't have to be strictly limited by that, which is good to know.

So, first things first, I need to figure out my own approach to this. As someone who's a bit more reflective, my initial instinct isn't to just start spitting out ideas immediately. I prefer to gather my thoughts, maybe do a little internal brainstorming, and try to build a foundational concept before I start discussing it openly. If I just blurt things out, they often feel half-baked to me, and I like my contributions to have some substance.

Given that, I think my best first step is to spend some time alone, just mulling over different possibilities. I'll probably start by jotting down some keywords or themes – not necessarily sci-fi related at first, just anything that sparks interest. Maybe think about different emotions or experiences I'd want players to have. Do I want something relaxing, something intense, something thought-provoking? What kind of core mechanic could support that? I'll probably explore a few different combinations of genres too, since they've given us such a wide range to consider. A mystery RPG with a life-sim element in space, for example, or a competitive puzzle game with a deep narrative. Just broad strokes to begin.

After I've spent some time developing a few potential core ideas – nothing too detailed, just enough to have a solid hook and a general direction – then I'll think about who to consult. Our department leader, Yamaguchi-san, is also reflective, so I think she'd appreciate a more thought-out initial proposal rather than just a flurry of disconnected thoughts. I'd definitely want to talk to her before pitching anything too broadly. She's also the one who'll be summarizing everything, so understanding her initial thoughts or any subtle nudges she might have received from higher up would be invaluable. Plus, if I'm going to eventually share my ideas with the more intuitive members like Kato-kun or Yamada-san, having refined them a bit first will make the conversation more productive. I want to be able to present something concrete enough to get meaningful feedback.

So, in short: first, a good chunk of solitary, structured brainstorming to lay out a few distinct conceptual paths. Then, I'll plan to consult with Yamaguchi-san to get her perspective and make sure I'm on a sensible track before engaging the wider team. That seems like the most effective way for me to contribute meaningfully to this free-form challenge. (formed immediately after interacting with the start of the project)

What if an ancient alien civilization left behind not technology, but... emotions? Or what if a future society lives entirely in a virtual reality, but the *real* world outside is actually a fantastical, magical realm they've forgotten?

Or a puzzle game where you're deciphering an alien language, and the puzzles themselves are integrated into the narrative and world-building?

I'll focus on a core concept first, something that feels fresh and exciting. Then I'll start building a story around it – what's the hook? Who are the characters? What's at stake?
